sources.notes: Statement and constraints: buy exactly n watermelons with at most k deals, where n and k are up to 1e9.
Official solution confirms ternary decomposition and splitting large deals into smaller deals.
Cross-checks that replacing one 3^x deal by three 3^(x-1) deals is cheaper and consumes two extra deals.
Independent Chinese write-up derives the saving and batch greedy replacement.

annotation.summary: Given n and a deal limit k, buy exactly n watermelons using deals of size 3^x and minimize total cost, or report impossible.
annotation.constraints: t <= 10000, n,k <= 1e9; only O(log_3 n) ternary digits can be processed per test.
annotation.core_idea: The minimum possible number of deals is the digit sum of n in base 3, obtained by buying each ternary digit separately. If this exceeds k, no solution exists. Otherwise, one deal of size 3^x can be split into three deals of size 3^(x-1), increasing the deal count by 2 and decreasing the cost. Splitting larger powers saves more, so greedily split from the largest ternary digit downward while the remaining deal budget allows.
tricks: Represent n by ternary digit counts of deal sizes.
Use extra deal budget in chunks of two.
Greedily split largest available powers first.

solution_variants.name: ternary-split-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Count ternary digits as initial deals, then batch-convert high powers to three lower powers using at most floor((k-min_deals)/2) splits.

tags.evidence: The feasible minimum-deal representation is exactly the base-3 digit expansion of n, and all later operations modify these digit counts.
tags.description: Use positional base representation of an integer to derive counts, carries, digit sums, or canonical decompositions; applies when the base digits are central to the solution rather than just input parsing.
tags.created_reason: Existing math/number-theory is too broad, while this problem is specifically solved by base-3 digit decomposition and reverse-carry style operations.

tags.evidence: After the ternary representation is fixed, the optimal use of extra deals is to split the largest remaining deal size first because it gives the largest cost reduction.
